text: Deep learning in single-cell and spatial transcriptomics data analysis: advances and challenges from a data science perspective. The development of single-cell and spatial transcriptomics has revolutionized our capacity to investigate cellular properties, functions, and interactions in both cellular and spatial contexts. Despite this progress, the analysis of single-cell and spatial omics data remains challenging. First, single-cell sequencing data are high-dimensional and sparse, and are often contaminated by noise and uncertainty, obscuring the underlying biological signal. Second, these data often encompass multiple modalities, including gene expression, epigenetic modifications, metabolite levels, and spatial locations. Integrating these diverse data modalities is crucial for enhancing prediction accuracy and biological interpretability. Third, while the scale of single-cell sequencing has expanded to millions of cells, high-quality annotated datasets are still limited. Fourth, the complex correlations of biological tissues make it difficult to accurately reconstruct cellular states and spatial contexts. Traditional feature engineering approaches struggle with the complexity of biological networks, while deep learning, with its ability to handle high-dimensional data and automatically identify meaningful patterns, has shown great promise in overcoming these challenges. Besides systematically reviewing the strengths and weaknesses of advanced deep learning methods, we have curated 21 datasets from nine benchmarks to evaluate the performance of 58 computational methods. Our analysis reveals that model performance can vary significantly across different benchmark datasets and evaluation metrics, providing a useful perspective for selecting the most appropriate approach based on a specific application scenario. We highlight three key areas for future development, offering valuable insights into how deep learning can be effectively applied to transcriptomic data analysis in biological, medical, and clinical settings.

The advancement of single-cell and spatial transcriptomics techniques has facilitated in-depth investigations of cellular characteristics, functions, and interactions, considering both cellular activity and spatial context within tissues. Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) quantifies gene expression at the cellular level, thereby elucidating cellular composition, gene expression patterns, and molecular characteristics. Recognized as the Method of the Year by Nature Methods in 2013, scRNA-seq has significantly advanced research into complex biological questions. However, tissue dissociation disrupts spatial cell distribution and intercellular interactions. Spatial transcriptomics (ST) generates spatially resolved transcriptomic data to create detailed tissue maps at the subcellular level, representing a significant advance by transitioning from cellular resolution to spatially sub-cellular resolution, named Method of the Year in 2020 by Nature Methods. Single-cell and ST are crucial for studying the microenvironment at cellular and spatial resolutions, respectively. However, the complexity of biological tissues and the limitations of current sequencing techniques present significant analytical challenges. Traditional analysis techniques for bulk RNA sequencing were not designed to capture single-cell heterogeneity and spatial context. As single-cell data increase in volume and complexity, traditional statistical methods struggle with high-dimensional and sparse data, facing challenges such as nonlinear relationships and computational complexity. Deep learning (DL) is a powerful tool for modeling large-scale, high-dimensional complex data, demonstrating versatility across numerous scientific domains. Recent reviews of DL applications in single-cell data have introduced methods such as multilayer perceptrons, autoencoders (AE), generative adversarial networks (GAN), convolutional neural networks (CNN), and graph neural networks (GNN). This review aims to discuss four major data science challenges: data sparsity (curse of dimensionality, noise, uncertainty), data diversity (multimodal and multi-source integration), data scarcity (missing annotations and modalities), and data correlation (spatiotemporal dependencies and prior knowledge), elucidating their origins, exploring potential solutions, and providing insights into underlying mechanisms.

Single-cell transcriptomes typically encompass tens of thousands of genes and exhibit considerable variability in expression across individual cells. Many genes remain inactive within a particular cell type, and even within the same cell type, certain genes may be transiently unexpressed due to the dynamic nature of the transcriptome and fluctuations in the cell cycle state. Consequently, gene expression matrices tend to be highly sparse. The curse of dimensionality arises from the exponential increase in the volume of space as the number of dimensions grows, leading to sparsity and making it more difficult to cover the space effectively with a limited number of observations. To address this, feature selection and dimensionality reduction techniques are employed. Traditional methods include parameterized approaches like principal component analysis (PCA) and nonparametric methods such as t-distributed stochastic neighbor embedding (t-SNE) and uniform manifold approximation and projection (UMAP). In contrast, DL methods, such as scvis which employs a variational autoencoder (VAE) framework, model relationships through explicit mathematical formulations. For a high-dimensional scRNA-seq dataset $\mathcal{D}=\left\{x_{n}\right\}_{n=1}^{N}$, scvis assumes the observed data are generated from a low-dimensional prior distribution $p\left(z_{n}\mid\theta\right)=\prod_{i=1}^{d}\mathcal{N}\left(z_{n, i}\mid 0, I\right)$ and a generative distribution $p\left(x_{n}\mid z_{n},\theta\right)$. The model is optimized to ensure that similar cells exhibit analogous posterior distributions, preserving global structure in the latent space. Biological noise arises from intrinsic randomness, while experimental noise reflects nonbiological fluctuations. Batch effects, a major source of noise, occur due to discrepancies in experimental conditions. Technical constraints often lead to missing values (dropout events), obscuring true biological signals. DL-based approaches like CLEAR use contrastive learning on augmented data to remove batch effects by mapping identical cell types from various experiments to a common latent space region. Imputation methods reconstruct missing values; for instance, DCA employs a VAE with a zero-inflated negative binomial (ZINB) noise model to infer parameters like mean, dispersion, and dropout probabilities, effectively separating noise from biological signal.

Data diversity refers to integrating information from various omics layers (genome, transcriptome, proteome) and multiple sources. A critical challenge is addressing scale discrepancies between different modalities, as variations in feature types, biological significance, and spatial distribution complicate alignment. Methods like LIGER and iNMF utilize Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF) to decompose multi-omics data into shared and modality-specific latent factors, preserving both common biological signals and dataset-specific variations. Models like scAI and MultiVI adopt VAE frameworks to generate modality-specific latent representations, averaging them to form a joint latent space. Another challenge is resolution mismatch, particularly when integrating ST with scRNA-seq. ST spots often cover multiple cells, whereas scRNA-seq provides single-cell resolution. Methods like Seurat, LIGER, and Harmony use mutual nearest neighbors (MNN) and shared latent representations to integrate these datasets. SoScope employs a multimodal DL framework integrating transcriptomic, histone, DNA, and protein data with high-resolution images, utilizing a variational Bayesian inference network to infer precise spatial maps. Modeling spatial information is crucial; frameworks like SpatialData, SSGATE, STAligner, and SpatialGlue model spatial dependencies. SpatialGlue, for instance, constructs spatial and feature neighbor graphs for each modality, using GNN encoders and attention layers to learn modality-specific graph representations and integrate them while preserving spatial relationships.

Data scarcity is a significant hurdle, primarily concerning missing data annotations and missing modalities. High-quality, large-scale annotated datasets are limited due to the labor and time required in lab settings. A key solution is the construction of simulation datasets using statistical modeling or DL. scDesign3 employs a Generalized Additive Model for Location, Scale, and Shape (GAMLSS) to generate single-cell multi-omics and ST data with known cell proportions and experimental designs. GlouNdGAN is a causal model-based GAN that simulates data aligned with Gene Regulatory Network (GRN) principles, featuring a Causal Controller for TFs and Target Generators for genes, allowing researchers to simulate interference by manipulating TF expression. Missing modalities pose another challenge; multi-channel sequencing is costly. DL solutions often rely on VAE architectures like totalVI, UniPort, POE, and MOE. These models learn joint latent spaces from single or dual modalities. For instance, totalVI trains on joint embeddings of two modalities with separate reconstruction, while UniPort trains a single-modality embedding to predict both. Modeling data correlation involves capturing complex spatiotemporal dependencies and incorporating prior knowledge. Gaussian Process-based models like MEFISTO model latent factors by incorporating temporal, spatial, and grouped data into the covariance function. Graph models, such as Markov Random Fields (MRF) used in Giotto, assume labels of neighboring cells exhibit similarity. DL frameworks like DeepLinc use GNNs to explore cell-cell interactions based on physical distance and gene expression. Incorporating prior biological knowledge (pathways, regulatory networks from databases like KEGG, Reactome) enhances accuracy and interpretability. GLUE integrates multi-omics data through a guidance graph, using variational posteriors to reconstruct the map, while methods like DeepCCI utilize databases such as LRIDB to define receptors and predict interactions.

The authors curated 21 datasets from nine benchmarks to evaluate 58 computational methods across tasks like imputation, integration, and cell-cell interaction prediction. Results indicate that performance varies significantly across datasets and metrics; for instance, DCA and scIGAN showed high imputation consistency, while scANVI demonstrated robustness in preserving biological consistency during multi-source integration. Future perspectives emphasize three key areas: 1) Innovative AI methods, such as foundational models (e.g., scMulan, scGPT) and agent-based methods (e.g., ReAct), which offer potential for interpretable inferences via self-supervised pretraining and chain-of-thought reasoning. 2) Development of fair benchmarks with biologically interpretable metrics, moving beyond simple accuracy scores to validate biological relevance through in vitro experiments and simulations. 3) Exploration of DL applications in practical scenarios, particularly in biology (embryonic and tissue development), precision medicine (disease heterogeneity, biomarker discovery, personalized therapies), and clinical practice (patient stratification, monitoring drug response). The review concludes that while DL offers powerful tools for single-cell and ST analysis, balancing interpretability with accuracy remains crucial, and establishing robust benchmarks is essential for evaluating algorithmic generalizability.
